column1: Copied from the latest tailored AI Readiness content pack.

column3: question_text
column4: question_guidance
column5: evidence_examples
column6: scoring_1_desc
column7: scoring_2_desc
column8: scoring_3_desc
column9: scoring_4_desc
column10: scoring_5_desc

column3: Has leadership clearly defined why the business wants to use AI?
column4: Confirm whether leadership has named specific business problems AI is meant to address, such as slow proposal turnaround, weak lead qualification, reporting delays, or service bottlenecks, rather than a broad desire to 'use AI'.
column5: board papers; strategy deck; annual operating plan; transformation roadmap; executive interview notes
column6: AI is discussed as a trend or brand signal with no defined business problem.
column7: Leadership refers to broad goals like innovation or efficiency, but no concrete operational or commercial targets are named.
column8: One or two business problems are referenced, but the rationale is not documented or consistently understood.
column9: Leadership has defined clear target problems and expected benefits for the main AI focus areas.
column10: A documented AI thesis links named business problems, target workflows, and measurable outcomes.

column3: Are AI opportunities linked to measurable business priorities?
column4: Check whether proposed AI use cases map directly to business priorities such as revenue growth, margin protection, service speed, compliance control, or cost-to-serve reduction.
column5: KPI tree; business scorecard; department goals; transformation business case; operating plan
column6: AI ideas have no visible link to strategic or operational priorities.
column7: A loose link is implied, but use cases are not tied to owned business metrics.
column8: Some use cases map to priorities, though the links are incomplete or uneven across functions.
column9: Most shortlisted use cases are tied to named business priorities and performance metrics.
column10: All priority use cases are explicitly linked to owned KPIs, expected impact, and sponsoring leaders.

column3: Has the business identified and ranked priority AI use cases?
column4: Look for a ranked shortlist that separates nice-to-have ideas from near-term pilot candidates using criteria such as value, feasibility, data readiness, risk, and time to value.
column5: use case register; prioritization matrix; workshop outputs; pilot shortlist
column6: No AI use cases have been identified or documented.
column7: A list of ideas exists, but there is no ranking or screening logic.
column8: A shortlist exists, but prioritization is subjective or missing key criteria such as risk or feasibility.
column9: Priority use cases have been ranked using practical business and delivery criteria.
column10: A maintained use case portfolio is ranked by value, feasibility, data readiness, risk, and ownership.

column3: Is there clear executive ownership for AI direction?
column4: Confirm whether one executive has authority to approve priorities, resolve trade-offs, and own AI direction across business and technology teams.
column5: governance chart; steering committee terms; role descriptions; decision log
column6: No executive owner is accountable for AI direction.
column7: Several leaders show interest, but accountability is diffuse and decision rights are unclear.
column8: An owner is informally recognized, but authority or sponsorship is weak.
column9: A clear executive sponsor owns direction and can make prioritization decisions.
column10: An accountable executive sponsor is formally mandated, active, and integrated into governance.

column3: Has budget or resource capacity been considered for AI initiatives?
column4: Assess whether the business has considered budget, people capacity, change effort, data work, and integration effort rather than only software subscription cost.
column5: budget pack; resourcing plan; vendor estimates; project portfolio; hiring plan
column6: No budget, capacity, or delivery effort has been considered.
column7: A tool budget may exist, but delivery effort, data work, and business capacity are not planned.
column8: Some resourcing has been discussed, but commitments are partial or optimistic.
column9: Budget and delivery capacity have been considered for the main pilot candidates.
column10: Funding, internal capacity, external support needs, and sequencing are clearly planned.

column3: Has the business distinguished between automation opportunities and true AI opportunities?
column4: Test whether the business can distinguish between fixing the process, automating rules, and applying AI judgment, generation, retrieval, or prediction.
column5: workflow redesign notes; automation inventory; use case screen; workshop outputs
column6: All inefficiencies are being labelled as AI opportunities.
column7: There is limited understanding of where rules-based automation would solve the problem better.
column8: Some teams distinguish automation from AI, but the logic is inconsistent.
column9: Most shortlisted opportunities have been correctly classified as process, automation, or AI.
column10: A clear decision method consistently separates process fixes, automation candidates, and true AI use cases.

column3: Is there a decision framework for evaluating new AI use cases?
column4: Review whether new ideas are assessed through a repeatable framework covering business value, feasibility, data dependency, integration complexity, risk, and ownership.
column5: screening template; intake process; governance checklist; use case scorecard
column6: No consistent method exists to assess new AI requests.
column7: Requests are evaluated ad hoc based on enthusiasm or vendor pressure.
column8: Basic evaluation criteria exist, but they are not consistently applied.
column9: A repeatable framework is used for most new AI proposals.
column10: A formal intake and screening process is consistently used and informs portfolio decisions.

column3: Is there a phased AI roadmap or pilot path aligned to business maturity?
column4: Check whether the roadmap sequences foundation work, automation cleanup, pilots, and scale in a way that matches current maturity rather than jumping straight to enterprise rollout.
column5: AI roadmap; pilot plan; transformation plan; stage-gate model
column6: No roadmap or pilot path exists.
column7: There is a tools-first aspiration, but no sequenced plan from readiness to rollout.
column8: A roadmap exists, but dependencies and maturity gates are only partly reflected.
column9: A phased roadmap aligns key pilots to readiness work and business priorities.
column10: A staged roadmap clearly sequences foundations, pilots, scale decisions, and operating model changes.

column3: Have target workflows for AI been clearly identified?
column4: Confirm that specific workflows have been named, bounded, and linked to departments, users, triggers, and outputs—for example proposal drafting, ticket triage, or meeting note capture.
column5: process inventory; workflow maps; operations interviews; use case register
column6: No specific workflows have been identified.
column7: Broad functions are named, but not the actual workflows where AI would be used.
column8: Some workflows are named, though boundaries, owners, or trigger points are still unclear.
column9: Priority workflows are clearly identified with enough detail to assess them.
column10: A documented workflow inventory links each AI candidate to owners, volume, users, and expected outcomes.

column3: Do candidate workflows involve repetitive interpretation, drafting, classification, or decision support?
column4: Assess whether the workflow genuinely contains AI-suitable tasks such as summarizing calls, drafting first-pass content, classifying requests, searching knowledge, or supporting judgment-based routing.
column5: task samples; SOPs; call notes; service queues; document examples
column6: The shortlisted workflows do not contain meaningful AI-suitable tasks.
column7: Only isolated or low-value tasks within the workflow would benefit from AI.
column8: Some tasks within the workflow fit AI, but the case is mixed.
column9: The workflow contains several repetitive cognitive tasks suited to AI support.
column10: The workflow is rich in repetitive interpretation, drafting, retrieval, or decision-support tasks that strongly suit AI.

column3: Are target workflows high enough in volume or business importance to justify AI?
column4: Evaluate whether the workflow matters enough in terms of transaction volume, labor intensity, customer impact, cost of delay, or revenue consequence to justify intervention.
column5: ticket volumes; turnaround data; SLA breaches; proposal counts; labor time analysis
column6: Selected workflows are too small, too rare, or too low-value to justify AI.
column7: The relevance is mostly anecdotal, with limited evidence of business importance.
column8: The workflow has moderate value or volume, but the commercial case is not yet strong.
column9: The workflow is materially important by volume, labor burden, or business impact.
column10: The workflow is a high-impact candidate with quantified volume, pain, and value potential.

column3: Are the pain points within target workflows clearly understood?
column4: Check whether the current pain is defined precisely—such as rework, slow turnaround, missed follow-up, inconsistent answers, backlog growth, or supervisor dependency.
column5: root-cause analysis; issue logs; escalation themes; workshop notes; quality reviews
column6: Pain points are vague or assumed, with no real understanding of the problem.
column7: Teams describe frustration, but not the exact failure points in the workflow.
column8: Some pain points are known, though root causes and impact are not consistently mapped.
column9: The main pain points and their operational effects are clearly understood.
column10: Pain points are mapped to workflow stages, root causes, and measurable business impact.

column3: Are target workflows documented well enough to redesign?
column4: Review whether the workflow is documented at a level that shows actors, sequence, systems used, decisions, handoffs, inputs, outputs, and exception paths.
column5: process map; SOP; swimlane; desk procedure; workflow screenshot pack
column6: The workflow is undocumented or only known informally.
column7: Only fragments of the workflow are documented, leaving major gaps.
column8: Core steps are documented, but handoffs, decisions, or exceptions are incomplete.
column9: The workflow is documented well enough to redesign and test an AI intervention.
column10: An end-to-end workflow view exists with roles, systems, handoffs, decisions, inputs, outputs, and exceptions.

column3: Are target workflows stable and repeatable rather than chaotic?
column4: Assess whether the workflow follows a stable pattern often enough for AI to perform consistently, even if some exceptions exist.
column5: variation analysis; supervisor interviews; case samples; quality audits
column6: The workflow changes case by case and is too chaotic for consistent AI use.
column7: There are frequent variations and workarounds that would make AI unreliable.
column8: The workflow has a repeatable core, but variation is still material.
column9: The workflow is mostly stable, with exceptions that can be managed.
column10: The workflow has a strong repeatable pattern and a well-understood exception profile.

column3: Has the business identified where basic automation is enough and AI is unnecessary?
column4: Look for evidence that the business has screened out tasks that should be handled through forms, rules, routing, templates, or workflow automation instead of AI.
column5: automation backlog; redesign workshop notes; solution options analysis
column6: The business is trying to use AI for tasks that are basic automation or process design issues.
column7: There is little discipline in deciding when AI is unnecessary.
column8: Some automation-only opportunities are recognized, but the distinction is incomplete.
column9: The business usually separates automation candidates from AI candidates correctly.
column10: The solution selection process consistently reserves AI for tasks that require interpretation, generation, retrieval, or prediction.

column3: Are there clear human review points in workflows where AI may be introduced?
column4: Confirm where human review sits in the workflow: who checks the output, at what stage, under what risk conditions, and what happens when the AI result is wrong or uncertain.
column5: approval matrix; SOP; QA checklist; escalation flow
column6: No human review points have been defined.
column7: Review is assumed, but no owner, trigger, or method is clear.
column8: Some review points exist, but they are inconsistent or incomplete.
column9: Human review is designed for the main workflows and risk points.
column10: Clear human-in-the-loop controls specify owners, triggers, escalation paths, and override rules.

column3: Is the data required for priority AI use cases already captured?
column4: Check whether the data inputs needed for the chosen use cases already exist in usable form, including structured fields, documents, tickets, call transcripts, or knowledge assets.
column5: data inventory; source system extracts; document repositories; transcript sources
column6: The core data needed for the priority use cases is not being captured.
column7: Some relevant data exists, but major required inputs are missing or unavailable.
column8: Most core inputs exist, though there are notable gaps or workarounds.
column9: The main inputs required for the priority use cases are available.
column10: The full set of core structured and unstructured inputs is already captured for priority use cases.

column3: Is business data stored in accessible systems rather than scattered informally?
column4: Assess whether data lives in systems that can be queried, exported, or connected, rather than in personal inboxes, local files, or uncontrolled shared folders.
column5: system screenshots; shared drive structure; inbox dependency review; data access map
column6: Important data is mostly trapped in personal or informal locations.
column7: Data is highly fragmented across files, inboxes, and isolated folders.
column8: There is a mix of system-based storage and informal storage that creates friction.
column9: Most important data is stored in accessible business systems or managed repositories.
column10: Core business data is centrally stored, accessible, and operationally retrievable.

column3: Are key data fields standardized across systems or teams?
column4: Review whether the same business fields—such as customer status, lead source, ticket category, product code, or job stage—mean the same thing across teams and systems.
column5: data dictionary; field mapping; CRM configuration; reporting definitions
column6: Critical fields are inconsistent or undefined across teams and systems.
column7: Some fields are standardized, but major differences still create ambiguity.
column8: Core fields are partly standardized, though important inconsistencies remain.
column9: Most critical fields use consistent definitions and structures.
column10: Key fields are standardized, governed, and consistently used across systems and teams.

column3: Is data quality sufficient for reliable AI outputs?
column4: Assess whether duplicates, missing values, stale records, misclassification, and poor document hygiene would materially degrade AI output quality.
column5: data quality audit; exception report; duplicate analysis; sample record review
column6: Data quality is poor enough to make AI outputs unreliable.
column7: Data quality issues are frequent and unresolved in key datasets.
column8: Quality is acceptable in parts, but important gaps would still affect pilots.
column9: Data quality is generally good for the priority use cases, with manageable issues.
column10: Data quality is actively controlled and strong enough to support reliable AI outputs.

column3: Is there enough historical data depth for the intended use cases?
column4: Check whether enough historical depth exists to support the intended use case, especially for trend analysis, forecasting, retrieval quality, pattern detection, or performance comparison.
column5: historic exports; archive depth review; reporting history; retention policy
column6: There is not enough history to support the intended use cases.
column7: Historical depth is very limited or patchy across key datasets.
column8: Some history exists, but depth is uneven or insufficient for certain use cases.
column9: Historical data depth is adequate for the main use cases under consideration.
column10: Strong historical depth exists across the main datasets needed for pilot and scale use cases.

column3: Are unstructured knowledge sources organized and searchable?
column4: Review whether policies, SOPs, FAQs, product information, case notes, and other unstructured content are organised, current, and searchable enough for retrieval-based AI.
column5: knowledge base; SharePoint structure; SOP library; FAQ repository; document taxonomy
column6: Unstructured knowledge is disorganized, outdated, or largely unusable.
column7: Some useful content exists, but it is hard to find, duplicated, or poorly maintained.
column8: Key content is partly organized, though gaps in structure or freshness remain.
column9: Most important knowledge assets are organised and searchable for pilot use.
column10: Knowledge assets are curated, current, tagged or structured, and ready for retrieval-based AI.

column3: Are data owners defined for critical datasets?
column4: Confirm whether named owners are responsible for data quality, definitions, access, and remediation for the datasets that matter to the AI use cases.
column5: RACI matrix; data owner register; role descriptions; stewardship model
column6: No one is clearly accountable for critical datasets.
column7: Ownership is informal and depends on individual goodwill.
column8: Some important datasets have owners, but accountability is incomplete.
column9: Most critical datasets have defined owners with practical responsibility.
column10: All priority datasets have clear owners responsible for quality, access, and definition control.

column3: Is there a reliable source of truth for customer, operational, or financial data?
column4: Assess whether the business has a trusted primary source for customer, operational, and financial facts rather than competing spreadsheets or conflicting reports.
column5: source-of-truth map; BI model; finance reporting logic; master data register
column6: There is no trusted source of truth for critical business data.
column7: Multiple conflicting versions of the same data are used across teams.
column8: A partial source-of-truth model exists, but some important areas still conflict.
column9: Trusted primary sources exist for most critical business data.
column10: The organisation uses clearly defined and widely accepted sources of truth across key domains.

column3: Are the core business systems documented and understood?
column4: Confirm whether the business can list its core systems, what each one does, which workflows depend on them, who administers them, and where major data lives.
column5: application inventory; architecture diagram; admin list; workflow-system map
column6: The core system landscape is not documented or understood.
column7: Only a partial view of the environment exists, with major blind spots.
column8: Most major systems are known, but workflow role, ownership, or dependencies are incomplete.
column9: Core systems are documented well enough to support pilot planning.
column10: A maintained system inventory shows purpose, ownership, data role, and workflow dependencies.

column3: Do key systems support integration methods needed for AI deployment?
column4: Assess whether the relevant systems expose APIs, webhooks, integration connectors, secure exports, or workflow tooling needed to move data and actions into or out of AI services.
column5: vendor docs; API references; middleware catalogue; admin settings; integration review
column6: Key systems offer no practical integration method for the target use cases.
column7: Integration options are highly limited or restricted in the systems that matter most.
column8: Some required systems support integration, but important gaps remain.
column9: Most priority systems support viable integration methods for pilots.
column10: The main systems supporting priority use cases have strong and practical integration options.

column3: Can AI outputs be inserted back into real workflows or systems?
column4: Check whether AI outputs can land where the user already works—such as CRM notes, ticket queues, proposal templates, inbox workflows, or approval tools—rather than living in a disconnected side app.
column5: workflow mockups; system fields; UI screenshots; process redesign notes
column6: AI outputs would remain outside the live workflow and require manual re-entry.
column7: Only limited insertion points exist, so outputs would still be operationally disconnected.
column8: Some outputs could be inserted into workflow tools, but coverage is incomplete.
column9: Main pilot workflows have practical insertion points for AI outputs.
column10: AI outputs can be embedded directly into the system steps where users already act.

column3: Are teams overly dependent on manual exports, imports, or copy-paste between systems?
column4: Measure how much the current process depends on CSV exports, spreadsheet updates, copy-paste, email forwarding, or manual rekeying between systems.
column5: handoff analysis; user interviews; screen recordings; ops walkthroughs
column6: The workflow is heavily dependent on manual transfers between systems.
column7: Manual exports and copy-paste are frequent and materially slow the process.
column8: There is a mix of manual and automated movement that still creates friction.
column9: Manual transfer points are limited and manageable for a pilot.
column10: Data and task movement across systems is largely automated or system-native.

column3: Are system handoffs and dependencies visible enough to support AI design?
column4: Review whether upstream and downstream dependencies are visible enough to show what triggers the workflow, where data enters, what breaks, and which teams or systems are affected by change.
column5: dependency map; service blueprint; integration flow; support incident themes
column6: System handoffs and dependencies are largely invisible.
column7: Only fragments of the dependency chain are understood.
column8: The main dependencies are known, but not all failure points or handoffs are mapped.
column9: Most system handoffs and dependencies are visible for the priority workflows.
column10: An end-to-end dependency view exists, including triggers, handoffs, failure points, and affected owners.

column3: Is there a practical technical path to implement the top AI use cases?
column4: Assess whether there is a realistic implementation path covering data flow, security, user touchpoint, integration approach, and delivery ownership for the top use cases.
column5: solution outline; architecture option paper; pilot design; technical discovery notes
column6: No practical implementation path has been identified.
column7: Possible paths are speculative and leave major technical questions unresolved.
column8: A workable path is visible, but some dependencies or constraints remain unclear.
column9: There is a credible technical path for the main pilot candidates.
column10: A well-defined technical path exists for priority pilots, with manageable dependencies and constraints.

column3: Is there technical ownership for integrations, automations, or AI deployment?
column4: Confirm whether technical ownership is assigned across solution design, integration build, security review, support, and post-launch maintenance.
column5: delivery RACI; IT operating model; project plan; support ownership matrix
column6: No one clearly owns technical delivery or support for AI-related work.
column7: Ownership depends on individuals rather than defined roles.
column8: Some technical ownership exists, but gaps remain across build, security, or support.
column9: Technical ownership is clear for most pilot activities.
column10: Named technical owners cover design, build, security, support, and change control.

column3: Are current systems robust enough to support pilot deployment without major disruption?
column4: Evaluate whether current systems can absorb a pilot without causing instability, major downtime risk, or unacceptable workarounds for users.
column5: incident trends; platform health reports; release constraints; admin interviews
column6: Current systems are too fragile or overloaded to support a pilot safely.
column7: A pilot would create high disruption risk because the environment is unstable.
column8: A pilot is possible, but reliability or support risk needs close management.
column9: Current systems are stable enough to support controlled pilot deployment.
column10: The environment is stable, supported, and operationally ready for pilot activity.

column3: Are target processes documented and repeatable?
column4: Review whether the target process is formally described and can be executed the same way by different people using the same inputs, outputs, and steps.
column5: SOP; desk instructions; playbook; process map; onboarding guide
column6: The process is undocumented and varies by person.
column7: A loose process exists, but it is not formally documented or reliably repeatable.
column8: Core steps are documented, though consistency still depends on individual practice.
column9: The process is documented and mostly repeatable across the team.
column10: The process is well-documented, stable, and repeatable across users and locations.

column3: Are teams executing the same process consistently?
column4: Assess whether teams follow the same business rules, thresholds, escalation paths, and completion standards when executing the process.
column5: quality reviews; supervisor observations; calibration notes; audit samples
column6: Different people or teams run the process in materially different ways.
column7: There are frequent inconsistencies in rules, quality, or handoff behavior.
column8: The process is consistent in core areas, but variation still appears in important cases.
column9: Teams execute the process mostly consistently against shared rules.
column10: Execution is highly consistent, calibrated, and monitored across the process owners.

column3: Are obvious rule-based steps already automated or at least identified?
column4: Look for rule-based steps that should already be solved with templates, routing rules, validations, or workflow automation before AI is added.
column5: automation inventory; BPM backlog; workflow redesign notes; system rules review
column6: Obvious automation candidates have not been identified and AI is being considered too early.
column7: Some rule-based tasks are known, but they remain largely untreated.
column8: Basic automation opportunities have been identified, though not consistently addressed.
column9: Most obvious rule-based steps have been identified and several have already been improved.
column10: Rule-based tasks are largely automated or intentionally excluded before AI is introduced.

column3: Are process inputs and outputs clearly defined?
column4: Confirm whether the process has defined inputs, expected outputs, acceptance criteria, and data handoff points so that AI has a clear operating frame.
column5: form design; SOP; template pack; control checklist
column6: Inputs and outputs are unclear or inconsistent.
column7: Some inputs and outputs are known, but the process still relies on interpretation.
column8: Key inputs and outputs are partly defined, though some ambiguity remains.
column9: Inputs, outputs, and handoffs are defined for most target processes.
column10: Inputs, outputs, acceptance criteria, and handoffs are clearly defined and controlled.

column3: Are exception paths understood and manageable?
column4: Assess whether exception scenarios are known, categorized, and handled through clear escalation or override logic instead of ad hoc intervention.
column5: exception log; escalation matrix; supervisor notes; quality issue tracker
column6: Exception handling is chaotic and largely unmanaged.
column7: Exceptions are frequent, but there is little consistent logic for handling them.
column8: Common exception types are known, but coverage or escalation rules are incomplete.
column9: Most meaningful exceptions are understood and can be managed operationally.
column10: Exception categories, thresholds, owners, and escalation paths are clearly defined.

column3: Do target processes rely heavily on tribal knowledge?
column4: Check whether success depends on undocumented know-how held by a few experienced staff, such as how to interpret requests, route cases, or judge completeness.
column5: expert interviews; onboarding material; shadowing notes; SOP gaps
column6: The process depends heavily on tribal knowledge with little documentation.
column7: A significant amount of know-how sits with a small group of experienced staff.
column8: Some undocumented know-how remains, though parts have been captured.
column9: Most critical know-how has been documented and shared across the team.
column10: The process has low dependency on tribal knowledge because operating logic is clearly documented.

column3: Has the business stabilized broken processes before trying to apply AI?
column4: Review whether the business has already fixed major process instability—such as duplicate handoffs, missing approvals, unclear ownership, or broken intake—before positioning AI as the answer.
column5: continuous improvement log; redesign plan; root-cause actions; ops review
column6: Broken or unstable processes are being targeted for AI without prior stabilisation.
column7: Major process weaknesses remain unresolved and would undermine AI performance.
column8: Some stabilisation work has happened, but significant weaknesses are still present.
column9: Most major process weaknesses have been addressed before AI design.
column10: The business has deliberately stabilised weak processes before considering AI augmentation.

column3: Can target processes support human oversight without breaking workflow speed?
column4: Assess whether human oversight can be inserted into the process without destroying cycle time, creating new queues, or overloading managers.
column5: timing analysis; QA design; reviewer capacity plan; approval SLA
column6: Human review would make the process too slow or operationally unworkable.
column7: Oversight is possible only with high friction, delays, or supervisor burden.
column8: A workable oversight model exists, but it may still create bottlenecks in some cases.
column9: Human oversight can be built into the process with manageable delay.
column10: Oversight fits cleanly into workflow timing, capacity, and quality control requirements.

column3: Are there rules for what data can and cannot be used with AI tools?
column4: Confirm whether staff know what company, customer, employee, financial, or regulated data may be used with approved AI tools and what is prohibited.
column5: AI acceptable-use policy; data handling standard; security awareness material; tool policy
column6: No rules exist on what data may be used with AI tools.
column7: There is informal caution, but no clear guidance staff can apply.
column8: Basic rules exist, though important edge cases or data classes are not covered.
column9: Clear usage rules cover the main data types and approved tool scenarios.
column10: A formal policy defines allowed use, prohibited use, approved tools, and handling rules by data type.

column3: Are sensitive data categories identified and protected in AI-related workflows?
column4: Assess whether sensitive data categories are formally identified and protected in workflows involving prompts, uploads, retrieval, storage, or model outputs.
column5: data classification policy; privacy controls; masking rules; access matrix
column6: Sensitive data categories are not identified or protected for AI use.
column7: Some awareness exists, but controls are weak or inconsistently applied.
column8: Important sensitive categories are known, though protection measures are incomplete.
column9: Most sensitive data classes are identified and protected in the main AI scenarios.
column10: Sensitive data classes are clearly classified with strong protection, restriction, and monitoring controls.

column3: Are human approval requirements defined for higher-risk AI outputs?
column4: Check whether higher-risk outputs—such as customer communications, financial commentary, legal content, HR decisions, or policy advice—require defined human approval before use.
column5: approval matrix; risk policy; SOP; quality control workflow
column6: No human approval rules exist for higher-risk AI outputs.
column7: Approval happens informally and depends on individual discretion.
column8: Some high-risk cases require review, but the rules are not complete or consistent.
column9: Clear approval requirements exist for most high-risk AI outputs.
column10: Approval rules are formally defined by output type, risk level, owner, and escalation path.

column3: Can AI-generated outputs be traced, reviewed, or audited?
column4: Review whether the business can trace which prompts, data sources, model outputs, reviewers, and final actions were involved when AI is used in a workflow.
column5: system logs; audit trail; version history; review records; platform settings
column6: AI activity cannot be traced or audited in a meaningful way.
column7: Only limited logs or records exist, leaving major accountability gaps.
column8: Some traceability exists, but it is incomplete across sources, outputs, or approvals.
column9: Most pilot-relevant AI activity can be traced and reviewed.
column10: A strong audit trail covers prompts, inputs, outputs, review actions, and final decisions.

column3: Are access controls in place for data and systems used by AI workflows?
column4: Assess whether least-privilege access, role separation, and secure permissions are already in place for the systems and datasets involved in AI workflows.
column5: role matrix; system permissions; IAM review; admin settings
column6: Access to relevant systems and data is poorly controlled.
column7: Basic permissions exist, but over-access or weak role separation is common.
column8: Controls are acceptable in parts, though some gaps remain in critical areas.
column9: Access controls are strong for most systems and data used in AI workflows.
column10: Role-based access and security controls are robust, monitored, and appropriate for AI deployment.

column3: Is there a process to assess vendor, model, or platform risk?
column4: Check whether external AI vendors, models, or platforms are screened for security, privacy, data retention, contractual, and operational risk before use.
column5: vendor checklist; procurement review; security questionnaire; legal review
column6: There is no process to assess AI vendors, models, or platforms.
column7: Reviews happen inconsistently and only after tools are already in use.
column8: A basic review process exists, but it is incomplete or inconsistently applied.
column9: Most relevant vendors and tools go through a structured risk review.
column10: A formal cross-functional process reviews vendor, model, privacy, security, and operational risk before approval.

column3: Are legal, compliance, or regulatory obligations understood for target use cases?
column4: Confirm whether legal, regulatory, contractual, or industry-specific obligations have been identified for the selected use cases, especially where customer data, employment decisions, finance, or communications are involved.
column5: legal memo; compliance review; contract obligations; regulatory checklist
column6: Relevant legal or compliance obligations have not been assessed.
column7: There is limited awareness of obligations, but no structured interpretation.
column8: Some obligations are understood, though not all priority use cases have been reviewed.
column9: Most key legal and compliance obligations have been identified for the selected use cases.
column10: A clear legal and compliance position exists for each priority use case and is reflected in design controls.

column3: Is there a defined governance owner or forum for AI risk oversight?
column4: Assess whether there is a named owner or forum that reviews AI risk, policy exceptions, pilot approvals, incidents, and ongoing control performance.
column5: governance committee charter; risk forum notes; RACI; policy owner register
column6: No governance owner or forum exists for AI risk oversight.
column7: Oversight is informal and reactive rather than structured.
column8: A partial owner or forum exists, but cadence or authority is weak.
column9: A clear owner or forum governs the main AI risks and decisions.
column10: An active governance structure reviews approvals, incidents, policy adherence, and control effectiveness on a defined cadence.

column3: Do leaders understand the realistic strengths and limits of AI?
column4: Check whether leaders understand where AI helps, where it fails, what needs human oversight, and why process/data readiness matters before rollout.
column5: leadership interviews; workshop outputs; executive briefing notes; strategy discussions
column6: Leaders largely view AI through hype, fear, or vendor claims rather than practical operating reality.
column7: Leadership understanding is limited and misses major constraints or risk factors.
column8: Leaders have a reasonable high-level view, but lack depth on delivery and operating implications.
column9: Leaders understand the main strengths, limits, and readiness conditions for AI.
column10: Leaders show practical understanding of AI capabilities, failure modes, governance needs, and adoption requirements.

column3: Are managers prepared to redesign workflows around AI support?
column4: Assess whether managers are ready to redesign roles, workflows, controls, and performance expectations when AI is introduced into day-to-day operations.
column5: manager interviews; operating model workshop; department plans; pilot readiness notes
column6: Managers are not prepared to change how work is organised around AI.
column7: Managers show low readiness and still treat AI as an extra tool rather than a workflow change.
column8: Some managers are prepared, but readiness is uneven across pilot areas.
column9: Most managers in pilot areas are prepared to adapt workflows and controls.
column10: Managers are actively engaged in redesigning workflows, roles, and oversight around AI support.

column3: Are target users likely to trust and use AI outputs appropriately?
column4: Review whether the intended users trust AI enough to use it, while also understanding when to challenge, correct, or escalate outputs.
column5: user interviews; readiness survey; pilot feedback; workshop notes
column6: Users are likely to reject or misuse AI outputs due to very low trust or understanding.
column7: Trust is weak and users lack confidence in when or how to rely on AI support.
column8: User readiness is mixed, with openness in some areas but skepticism or confusion in others.
column9: Users are generally open to AI support and understand the basics of responsible use.
column10: Users show healthy trust, know when to review outputs, and are likely to use AI appropriately in workflow.

column3: Is resistance to workflow change likely to block adoption?
column4: Assess whether workflow change is likely to be blocked by fear of job impact, quality concerns, control concerns, manager resistance, or past change fatigue.
column5: stakeholder map; interview themes; change risk log; pulse survey
column6: Resistance is likely to materially block rollout in the target areas.
column7: Resistance is high and there is no clear plan to address it.
column8: There is some resistance, but it appears manageable with focused change work.
column9: Resistance is limited and can be addressed through normal enablement efforts.
column10: Change resistance is low and the organisation shows good readiness to adapt operating practices.

column3: Are training plans in place for teams affected by AI changes?
column4: Check whether training is planned for affected users, reviewers, managers, and support teams, covering both tool usage and new workflow responsibilities.
column5: training plan; enablement deck; role-based curriculum; onboarding plan
column6: No training plan exists for affected teams.
column7: Training has been mentioned, but not designed around roles or workflow impact.
column8: Some training is planned, though scope or audience coverage is incomplete.
column9: A clear training approach exists for the main pilot roles.
column10: Role-based training is fully planned across users, reviewers, managers, and support teams.

column3: Is there a clear owner for AI adoption, not just technology delivery?
column4: Confirm whether someone owns adoption outcomes such as usage, compliance with the new process, feedback capture, and correction of low uptake—not just technical launch.
column5: project governance; adoption KPI owner; RACI; change plan
column6: No one owns adoption after the tool is introduced.
column7: Adoption is assumed to happen naturally once the technology is available.
column8: Partial adoption ownership exists, but responsibilities are not complete.
column9: A clear owner is accountable for adoption in the main pilot areas.
column10: A named owner is accountable for adoption metrics, feedback loops, and corrective action.

column3: Do teams understand how human oversight will work in practice?
column4: Assess whether teams understand what they are expected to review, when they must override AI, what quality standard applies, and who is accountable for final decisions.
column5: workflow guide; SOP; QA framework; reviewer training notes
column6: Teams do not understand how oversight should work in practice.
column7: Oversight expectations are vague and likely to create inconsistent behavior.
column8: Basic oversight roles are defined, but clarity is still uneven across teams.
column9: Most affected teams understand their oversight role and decision responsibility.
column10: Oversight responsibilities, review triggers, escalation, and accountability are clearly understood across teams.

column3: Is cross-functional collaboration strong enough to support AI implementation?
column4: Check whether business, operations, data, risk, and technology teams can work together quickly enough to design, approve, deploy, and improve AI use cases.
column5: governance cadence; delivery squad structure; issue log; operating rhythm
column6: Cross-functional collaboration is too weak or siloed to support implementation.
column7: Important teams work separately and coordination issues are likely to delay delivery.
column8: Collaboration works in some areas, but coordination is still inconsistent.
column9: The key teams can generally collaborate effectively on pilot delivery.
column10: Cross-functional collaboration is strong, with clear forums, decision paths, and delivery rhythm.

column3: Are baseline metrics available for target workflows?
column4: Confirm whether current baseline measures exist for the selected workflows, such as turnaround time, backlog, conversion, error rate, QA score, handle time, or cost per task.
column5: dashboard; workflow report; SLA report; finance analysis; quality scorecard
column6: No baseline performance data exists for the target workflows.
column7: Only anecdotal or incomplete baseline information is available.
column8: Some useful baseline metrics exist, but not enough for strong comparison.
column9: Baseline metrics are available for most of the main pilot workflows.
column10: A strong baseline exists across the key performance measures needed to assess pilot impact.

column3: Has success been clearly defined for each priority AI use case?
column4: Review whether success is defined per use case in concrete terms, such as reducing turnaround time by a target percentage, improving win rate, lowering rework, or increasing knowledge retrieval accuracy.
column5: pilot charter; business case; KPI definition sheet; sponsor approval
column6: Success has not been defined for the priority use cases.
column7: Success is described vaguely, for example as being faster or more efficient.
column8: Some measurable success criteria exist, but not for every main use case.
column9: Clear success criteria exist for most priority use cases.
column10: Each priority use case has explicit, measurable, approved success criteria.

column3: Can impact be measured in operational or commercial terms?
column4: Check whether the business can translate AI impact into operational and commercial terms such as labor saved, backlog reduced, SLA improvement, conversion uplift, margin gain, or risk reduction.
column5: value model; finance case; operations KPI map; service value analysis
column6: The business cannot express AI impact in measurable business terms.
column7: Impact is discussed conceptually, but not translated into owned metrics or value drivers.
column8: Some impact pathways are defined, though the model is incomplete.
column9: The main use cases have a workable operational and commercial impact model.
column10: Impact can be measured in clear business terms and tied to named value drivers.

column3: Are pilot KPIs defined before implementation starts?
column4: Assess whether pilot KPIs are agreed before launch, including adoption, quality, efficiency, control, and business outcome measures where relevant.
column5: pilot scorecard; launch checklist; governance pack; test plan
column6: Pilot KPIs are not defined before implementation.
column7: A few measures are discussed, but there is no agreed scorecard.
column8: A partial KPI set exists, though gaps remain in quality, control, or business outcome measures.
column9: A clear KPI scorecard exists for the main pilots before launch.
column10: Pilots have a fully defined pre-launch KPI scorecard covering outcome, efficiency, quality, control, and adoption.

column3: Is there a method to compare pre-AI and post-AI performance?
column4: Confirm whether there is a disciplined method to compare performance before and after AI, including timing, control group or baseline choice, and review cadence.
column5: evaluation method; baseline plan; PMO scorecard; experiment design
column6: There is no method to compare pre-AI and post-AI performance.
column7: Comparison would be ad hoc and vulnerable to subjective interpretation.
column8: A basic comparison method exists, but it lacks rigor or consistency.
column9: There is a clear method to compare pilot results against baseline performance.
column10: A formal evaluation approach defines baseline, review cadence, comparison method, and evidence standards.

column3: Are scaling thresholds defined for successful pilots?
column4: Check whether the business has defined the threshold for scaling, redesigning, pausing, or stopping a pilot based on evidence rather than enthusiasm.
column5: stage-gate criteria; governance pack; pilot decision framework
column6: No scale or stop criteria exist for successful or unsuccessful pilots.
column7: Scaling decisions would likely be made informally or subjectively.
column8: Some decision criteria exist, but thresholds or governance triggers are incomplete.
column9: Clear scaling criteria exist for the main pilots.
column10: Formal scale, redesign, and stop thresholds are defined and tied to governance decisions.

column3: Are owners accountable for tracking AI outcomes after deployment?
column4: Confirm whether named owners are responsible for tracking outcomes after launch, maintaining the KPI view, and escalating underperformance or control failures.
column5: owner register; KPI governance; PMO cadence; business review pack
column6: No one is accountable for tracking AI outcomes after launch.
column7: Outcome tracking depends on goodwill and is not assigned as an owned responsibility.
column8: Some accountability exists, but ownership is fragmented or incomplete.
column9: Clear owners track outcomes for most pilot and live AI use cases.
column10: Named owners are accountable for measurement, reporting, and corrective action after deployment.

column3: Can the business estimate financial value for the top AI use cases?
column4: Assess whether the business can estimate financial value using assumptions grounded in throughput, labor cost, revenue conversion, service capacity, error reduction, or risk avoidance.
column5: financial model; ROI estimate; sponsor business case; operations economics
column6: No financial value estimate exists for the priority AI use cases.
column7: Value estimates are rough, unsupported, or based mainly on vendor claims.
column8: A basic financial estimate exists, but key assumptions are weak or incomplete.
column9: The business has a credible financial estimate for the main use cases.
column10: Finance-backed value estimates exist for priority use cases and are tied to explicit operating assumptions.
